macro_segments.title: Introduction to Nedarim 68: Review of Father-Husband Partnership in Annulling Vows of a Na'arah Murasah

micro_segments.title: The Status of a Na'arah (Minor Girl) Before and After Kiddushin
How Chazal Read the Torah Sections on Vow Annulment to Establish Father-Husband Partnership
Two Key Principles: Pre-Kiddushin Vows and the Requirement of a Father

macro_segments.title: The Braita from Tanya Devei Rabbi Ishmael: Two Categories of Vows a Husband Can Annul

micro_segments.title: The Braita's Reading of 'Bein Ish L'Ishto' and 'Bein Av L'Vito' to Identify Na'arah Murasah
The Explicit Category of Inuy Nefesh (Self-Affliction Vows) from the Torah Text
The Derived Category of Devarim Shebeino L'Veinah (Vows Affecting the Relationship)
Debate Between Ran and Rambam: Do These Categories Apply to the Father?

macro_segments.title: The Question of Hafara Mechanics: Does One Party Cutting Half (Megez Gayiz) or Weakening (Miklesh Kalesh)?

micro_segments.title: Formulation of the Question: Does Each Act of Hafara Have Independent Effect?
Concrete Example: Two Olives and Whether Eating Them Results in Malkos
Student Objection: Why Assume Either Party's Hafara Has Effect Without the Other?

macro_segments.title: Proof from Braita: The Father as Anchor in the Partnership (Husband Dies vs. Father Dies)

micro_segments.title: Establishing the Principle: When Husband Dies, Father Gains Full Rights; When Father Dies, Husband Cannot Annul
Case 1: Husband Hears the Vow, Does Hafara or Kiyum, Then Dies—Father Can Now Annul
Three Scenarios Where Husband Does Not Problematically Interfere Before Dying
Bad Scenarios: Husband Does Kiyum (Confirms Vow) Before Dying—Father Cannot Later Annul

macro_segments.title: When Father Dies: Husband Cannot Annul, Even if Father Had Done Hafara

micro_segments.title: Father Hears and Does Active Hafara Before Dying—Husband Still Cannot Complete It
New Vow After Father Dies: Husband Still Cannot Annul Until Nisuin (Marriage)
Principle: Husband Needs Shutafut (Partnership) with Father; Cannot Act Alone

macro_segments.title: Complex Case: Father Does Hafara, Then Husband Dies—Can Father Take Over Husband's Portion?

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Father Heard and Did Hafara, Husband Died Without Ever Hearing
Beis Shammai vs. Beis Hillel: Can Father Annul the Husband's Portion?
Beis Shammai: Father Can Go Back and Annul the Husband's Portion (Chozer Ha'av)
Rabbi Natan Explains: This Is Beis Shammai's Position, Not Beis Hillel's
Beis Hillel: Father Cannot Annul What the Husband Would Have Done (Ein Yachol Lehafer)

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Resolution: The Debate Hinges on Hafara Mechanics—Megez Gayiz vs. Miklesh Kalesh

micro_segments.title: Gemara Concludes: Beis Shammai Holds Megez Gayiz (Cutting in Half); Beis Hillel Holds Miklesh Kalesh (Weakening)
Ran's Explanation: Megez Gayiz Allows Inheritance (Yerusha) of the Right; Miklesh Kalesh Does Not
Beis Shammai Logic: Half the Vow Is Annulled, Half Remains—A Concrete Thing That Can Be Inherited
Beis Hillel Logic: Weakening Is Not Substantial Enough to Inherit as a Right; Vow Stays Weak

topical_tags.term: Yesomah Dechaye Aviha (Orphan Controlled by Father)
